trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline stage complete with score 61.10 (runtime 3.05ms vs 3.41ms reference, 1.11x speedup). Profiling shows ConvTranspose3d GEMM (28.5%), memory transposes (35.4%), maxpool (12.3%), and elementwise ops (22.2%). Advancing to optimization stage.

nudges.text: You haven't established a baseline score yet. Instead of debugging a complex fused Triton kernel, first create a `ModelNew` that simply wraps the same PyTorch operations (ConvTranspose3d, scale, MaxPool3d, AdaptiveAvgPool3d, clamp) to get a passing correctness check and baseline score. Once you have a baseline, you can profile and then optimize with Triton kernels incrementally.

You have a baseline of 60.0. Before spending more time debugging a custom Triton kernel, try `torch.compile` on the `ModelNew.forward` method as a quick optimization — it can often fuse the scale, maxpool, GAP, and clamp automatically. If that gives a boost, you can iterate from there. Keep the simple PyTorch baseline as a fallback.

Your working baseline (score 60.0) was overwritten by a broken Triton kernel. Revert `generated_kernel.py` to the simple PyTorch wrapper that passed correctness, then try adding `@torch.compile` to the forward method as a quick win before attempting a manual Triton kernel. Run the benchmark to record an actual score.

Save a backup of your working 61.1 solution (`cp /workspace/generated_kernel.py /workspace/generated_kernel_backup.py`) before overwriting it with untested Triton kernels. Debug the Triton kernel in a separate file (e.g., `debug_kernel.py`) and only copy it to `generated_kernel.py` once it passes correctness.

You have 61.2 with `torch.compile`. Two quick things to try: (1) Fuse the scale multiplication into the ConvTranspose3d weights (`self.conv_transpose.weight.data *= self.scale`) in `__init__` to eliminate one kernel entirely. (2) Try `torch.compile(mode="max-autotune")` instead of default mode. Both are easy experiments before going back to manual Triton.

Fusing scale into weights failed because you also need to scale the bias: `self.conv_transpose.bias.data *= self.scale`. The conv output is `W*x + b`, so `scale*(W*x + b) = (scale*W)*x + (scale*b)`. Try that fix — it should give identical results and eliminate one kernel.
